threads.title: The Court Removal Project
threads.description: Three of today's five stories are about the same structural move: the executive branch eliminating or degrading judicial review of its decisions. TPS cancellations with no court review, university funding frozen pending a case moved to a friendlier court, and a VRA interpretation that forecloses the legal remedy states were court-ordered to use. The common thread is not immigration or education policy. It is the systematic removal of the judiciary as a check on executive action in specific domains.

connections.relationship: Both cases involve executive actions that depend on stripping or avoiding judicial review: TPS by statutory jurisdiction arguments, Harvard by moving to a contracts court where the constitutional claims are weaker.

The VRA ruling eliminates the judicial remedy for racial vote dilution at exactly the moment the TPS ruling may eliminate judicial oversight of mass deportation. Together they remove courts from two of the most consequential decisions about who belongs in American political and civic life.

Both use civil rights law as the nominal justification for an outcome that weakens civil rights protections in practice: the Harvard case uses antisemitism enforcement to punish institutional independence, the VRA ruling uses Equal Protection to make racial remediation unconstitutional.

threads.title: The Beijing Pivot Point
threads.description: The Iran ceasefire and China's new trade law are not separate stories. China is Iran's primary oil customer, the Iran war is driving oil above $112, China's new trade law directly targets companies complying with US sanctions on Iranian oil buyers, and Trump visits Beijing in two weeks. The Iran endgame almost certainly runs through Xi Jinping. The trade law is both a bargaining chip for the summit and a warning about what happens if no deal is reached.

connections.relationship: China's law explicitly protects Hengli Petrochemical, sanctioned for buying Iranian oil, and authorizes retaliation against US firms complying with those sanctions. China's willingness to absorb sanctioned Iranian oil is what makes the US maximum-pressure strategy against Iran structurally weaker than in prior conflicts, which explains why the Iran war has not produced a faster Iranian capitulation.
